meta.description: Universal definitions for reasons a person starts boxing. These definitions include metadata used by future systems (soul trait affinities, ambition biases, note on the psychological arc). Nation-specific files assign weights and local descriptions — this file is the canonical source of what each reason means mechanically and narratively. The moment system and ambition system will read these definitions when they are built.

reasons.label: Needed an Outlet
reasons.note: The anger or pain that drove them to boxing can sustain a long career — or, if the underlying wound never heals, become a liability under pressure.

reasons.label: Had Something to Prove
reasons.note: The chip never fully goes away. Their ambition is tied to external validation — which is fuel, but also risk if the validation never comes.

reasons.label: Saw It as a Way Out
reasons.note: The most motivated fighters at the start. The danger is that once they've escaped, the hunger sometimes goes with the escape.

reasons.label: Pure Passion for the Sport
reasons.note: Passion-driven fighters tend to last longer and train harder — the sport is its own reward. Less likely to burn out, less likely to cut corners.

reasons.label: Fell Into It
reasons.note: No strong pull in any direction. Their trajectory depends almost entirely on what they find at the gym and who coaches them.

reasons.label: Family Tradition
reasons.note: family_tradition fighters often struggle if they fail to live up to legacy. The same pride that drives them can break them.

reasons.label: Community Identity
reasons.note: community_identity fighters get something extra from a crowd. The occasion lifts them.

reasons.label: A Friend Brought Me
reasons.note: The simplest origin. The friend may have quit. They didn't.
